subject: Physics
title: Supercooling Water
description: A tiny lesson with a big payoff: Supercooling Water.

hook.text: You can cool water below 0°C without it freezing—if you're very careful. One bump and it freezes instantly.

buildup.text: Water needs a nucleation point—a tiny impurity or scratch—to start forming ice crystals.

discovery.text: Very pure water in a smooth container can stay liquid down to −48°C. It's called supercooling.

twist.text: Tap the bottle and the entire thing crystallizes in seconds. The trigger cascades through the liquid.

climax.text: Supercooled water droplets in clouds cause dangerous aircraft icing when they freeze on contact with wings.

punchline.text: Liquid below freezing. One touch and it instantly turns to ice.

quiz.question: Why doesn't supercooled water freeze?
options: It lacks a nucleation point to start ice crystal formation
